query: drugs used in urea cycle disorders to reduce ammonia

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for UCDs, or any IEM, should be done in consultation with an appropriate genetics/metabolic expert. For neonates with hyperammonemia, the immediate treatment goal is a rapid lowering of ammonia and reducing dietary protein intake. Hemodialysis is very effective at reducing plasma ammonia and should immediately be initiated if elevated hyperammonemia is observed. Ammonia scavenger medications such as Ammonul IV are also useful. Ammonul IV acts by removing glycine and glutamate from plasma thereby their reducing contribution to ammonia formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperammonemia -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment to Lower Ammonia
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with masked urea cycle defects develop hyperammonemia in conditions of increased stress and should be treated when levels rise greater than 3 times the upper limit of normal. Calories should be provided intravenously by glucose solutions, and all nitrogen intake should be stopped. Intravenous sodium benzoate and phenylacetate should be started. Dialysis should be considered only when ammonia levels do not fall after 8 hours of continual treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: N-carbamylglutamate (Carbaglu) is an analog of N-acetylglutamate, and small clinical trials have found it to improve ureagenesis in NAGS deficiency and late-onset CPS1 deficiency [5] . [6] [7] More extensive clinical trials are underway.

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ammonia is extremely toxic, particularly to the central nervous system (CNS). Newborns with severe mutations in any one of the first four enzymes of the urea cycle (see figure 1 and below) can become catastrophically ill within 36 to 48 hours of birth despite appearing normal at birth. It is possible, therefore, that a newborn can be discharged from the hospital before signs of UCDs develop and subsequently develop signs that go unrecognized at home. Hyperammonemia is key in diagnosing UCDs, and treatment should not be delayed while trying to reach a final diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Urea Cycle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The liver catabolizes toxic ammonia into urea, as shown in figure 1.  This energy-dependent process occurs only within the liver’s mitochondria and cytoplasm. When this process is not working efficiently, toxic ammonia (NH3) accumulates within the body and may elicit clinical manifestations such as lethargy, slurred speech, cerebral edema, and asterixis. Here, we will review the underlying physiology of the urea cycle. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Urea cycle disorders are inborn errors of metabolism resulting from defects in one of the enzymes or transporter molecules involved in the hepatic removal of ammonia from the bloodstream. Removal of ammonia from the bloodstream normally occurs via its conversion to urea, which is then excreted by the kidneys. Consequently, urea cycle disorders lead to an accumulation of ammonia. Ammonia is extremely toxic, particularly to the central nervous system. Newborns with severe mutations in any one of the first four enzymes of the urea cycle can become catastrophically ill within 36 to 48 hours of birth despite appearing normal at birth. It is, therefore, possible for a newborn to be discharged from the hospital before signs of urea cycle disorders develop. The newborn may subsequently develop signs that go unrecognized at home. Hyperammonemia is key to the diagnosis of urea cycle disorders, and treatment should not be delayed while a definitive diagnosis is sought. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of urea cycle disorders and highlights the role of interprofessional team members in collaborating to provide well-coordinated care and enhance outcomes for affected patients and families.

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders are complex and best done with an interprofessional team that includes a geneticist, pathologist, pediatrician, nephrologist, internist, biochemist, and dialysis nurse/nurse practitioner. Treatment for UCDs, or any IEM, should be done in consultation with an appropriate genetics/metabolic expert. For neonates with hyperammonemia, the immediate treatment goal is a rapid lowering of ammonia and reducing dietary protein intake. Hemodialysis is very effective at reducing plasma ammonia and should immediately be initiated if elevated hyperammonemia is observed. Ammonia scavenger medications such as an IV nitrogen binding agent are also useful. These agents act by removing glycine and glutamate from plasma, thereby reducing their contribution to ammonia formation. The prognosis for most of these infants is guarded. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperammonemia -- Treatment / Management -- Dietary Management
retrieved_docs.content: It is logical to assume protein intake needs to be restricted in patients with hyperammonemia. Protein restriction is not recommended, specifically in patients with chronic liver disease as they are already malnourished, and the general consensus is to continue a normal intake of proteins. Some compounds may play a role in lowering ammonia and can be supplemented in the diet. L-carnitine can be used to decrease the frequency of attacks in patients with urea cycle defects. [9] L-ornithine-L-aspartate may be of use in hepatic encephalopathy as it increases ammonia metabolism in the muscle. [27] Arginine should be supplemented in the diet of patients with urea cycle defects as it becomes an essential amino acid.

retrieved_docs.title: Ornithine Transcarbamylase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment of Hyperammonemia
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis may be necessary to control hyperammonemia, particularly in cases of severely elevated ammonia levels or if there is no response to intravenous sodium phenylacetate and sodium benzoate therapy. Hemodialysis is the most effective way of rapidly eliminating excess ammonia. An emergent need for hemodialysis is warranted if the serum ammonia level exceeds 500 µmol/L. Dialysis may be considered for lower levels if there is an inadequate clinical response after 4 hours of medical management. A serum ammonia level above 300 µmol/L will probably require hemodialysis for proper correction and should be planned accordingly with the initiation of parenteral nitrogen scavenger therapy in the interim. If the ammonia level falls between 100 to 300 µmol/L and the patient exhibits severe encephalopathy or seizures with a poor response to nitrogen scavengers, dialysis should be strongly considered. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There has been considerable progress in understanding the pathophysiology of UCDs, which may ultimately promote better medical interventions. [4] Hyperammonemia is a key etiological factor in UCDs and plays a key role in CNS toxicity. Although complex, some important concepts are emerging. The toxic effects of ammonia on the CNS are more severe in the developing brain than in the adult brain. Whether the toxic effects of hyperammonemia are reversible or not depends upon the exposure time, the dose of hyperammonemia, and the stage of neurological development. In the neonatal brain, hyperammonemia results in edema caused by swelling of astrocytes. The subsequent frequency and degree of swelling are key factors in determining the severity of CNS neurological disorders such as seizures, coma, and cognitive/motor deficits.

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain why the potential for urea cycle disorders poses a risk to early discharge of seemingly healthy newborns. Explain how high serum ammonia levels should be managed in a newborn. Explain the management of urea cycle disorders. Explain the importance of improving coordination amongst the interprofessional team to enhance care for patients affected by urea cycle disorders. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperammonemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperammonemia is a metabolic condition characterized by the raised levels of ammonia, a nitrogen-containing compound. Normal levels of ammonia in the body vary according to age. Hyperammonemia can result from various congenital and acquired conditions in which it may be the principal toxin. Hyperammonemia may also occur as a part of other disorders that involve various other metabolic abnormalities. Normally, ammonia is produced in the colon and small intestine from where it is transported to the liver to be converted to urea via the urea cycle. Urea, a water-soluble compound, can then be excreted via the kidneys. Ammonia levels rise if the liver is unable to metabolize this toxic compound as a result of an enzymatic defect or hepatocellular damage. The levels may also rise if portal blood is diverted to the systemic circulation, bypassing the liver, or there is increased production of ammonia due to an infection with certain microorganisms. [1] [2] Hyperammonemia in adults is most commonly related to cirrhotic liver disease in 90% of the cases. This metabolic abnormality, however, can be seen in numerous other disorders that need to be understood. Ammonia is a potent neurotoxin, and elevated levels in the blood can cause neurological signs and symptoms that may be acute or chronic, depending on the underlying abnormality. Hyperammonemia should be recognized early and treated immediately to prevent the development of life-threatening complications such as cerebral edema and brain herniation. Treatment strategies vary according to etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Urea Cycle -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperammonemia is toxic to the brain and leads to encephalopathy, which can manifest as cerebral edema, vomiting, blurred vision, asterixis, and seizures. Excess ammonia will also result in the increased formation of glutamine. Recall that glutamine synthetase uses the reactants NH and glutamate to yield glutamine. Glutamate is an excitatory neurotransmitter, and therefore decreased levels of glutamate will cause depressed neural activity, which manifests as lethargy or a comatose state. In addition, excess ammonia hinders the TCA cycle by causing alpha-ketoglutarate to form glutamate. Here we will discuss the most common urea cycle disorders:

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Newborns with UCDs typically appear normal at birth and shortly after that can present with nonspecific signs and symptoms similar to many IEMs or an infection, ie, lethargy, poor appetite, vomiting, and irritability. IEMs, in general, should be considered in the differential diagnosis of any infant with such signs. Except for arginase deficiency, most infants with severe UCDs rapidly develop cerebral edema and subsequent neurological problems resulting from acute hyperammonemia. Unless treated, hyperammonemia from severe UCDs can progress to coma and death. In cases of mild UCDs, hyperammonemia can result from a variety of stressors, such as illness and surgery [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Urea Cycle Disorders -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A key biomarker for UCDs is hyperammonemia (> 150 micromolar for a neonate and > 100 micromolar for an infant) in the absence of high anion gap and with a normal plasma glucose level.  In addition to plasma ammonia, it is useful to obtain plasma amino acids and plasma lactic acid if metabolic acidosis is present.  The laboratory finding characteristic of UCDs includes (a) elevated levels of plasma glutamine and alanine, (b) decreased plasma arginine (except for arginase deficiency), and (c) high or low plasma citrulline levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Arginase Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: This initial stage is critical, as there are other types of urea cycle disorders that benefit from arginine administration to reduce ammonia levels by indirectly increasing citrulline levels; at the same time, it attaches ammonia molecules for excretion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both therapeutic agents that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea) and اضطراب معدي (gastrointestinal disorder). Each of these bismuth compounds shares the same indicated uses, suggesting they have comparable efficacy in managing diarrheal conditions and related gastrointestinal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1900
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate may be used to treat إِمْساك (constipation), and إِمْساك is clinically associated with تَقَيُّء (vomiting). This relationship indicates that alleviating constipation with magnesium citrate could also impact the occurrence or severity of vomiting, highlighting the interconnected nature of gastrointestinal symptoms and the therapeutic role of magnesium citrate in managing constipation‑related clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2587
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea) and اضطراب مَعِدِيّ (gastrointestinal disturbance). Both compounds share the same clinical indications, offering similar benefits for managing these gastrointestinal conditions. However, each is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (renal impairment), meaning that patients with renal disease should not receive either bismuth subnitrate or bismuth subcitrate. Recognizing the parallel efficacy and shared contraindication of these bismuth salts is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: entities illustrate the diverse therapeutic roles of magnesium salts, iron compounds and fibrates, while emphasizing common safety considerations—particularly the avoidance of magnesium‑based agents and fibrates in patients with renal impairment and the avoidance of fibrates in hepatic steatosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: Clindamycin is contraindicated in patients with hepatic failure (الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيّ), meaning the drug should not be administered to individuals suffering from liver failure due to the risk of worsening hepatic function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 288
retrieved_docs.content: Osmotically acting laxatives comprise agents such as magnesium citrate and lactulose, both of which increase intestinal water content to facilitate bowel movements. Magnesium citrate, while effective as an osmotic laxative, is contraindicated in patients with intestinal perforation (ثقب معوي) because its osmotic activity could exacerbate the condition. Lactulose, also a member of the osmotic laxative class, does not share this specific contraindication and is commonly used to treat constipation and hepatic encephalopathy. Understanding the class membership of these agents and the specific contraindication of magnesium citrate helps clinicians select appropriate therapy and avoid adverse outcomes in patients with compromised intestinal integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1891
retrieved_docs.content: Aktivní uhlí (activated charcoal) is a medical adsorbent commonly employed for gastrointestinal decontamination after ingestion of toxins, while اليوريمية (uremia) is a metabolic complication of severe renal failure that typically requires renal replacement therapies such as dialysis. Activated charcoal is not an appropriate or effective treatment for uremia, and the two concepts are not directly therapeutic to one another; instead, uremia is managed with modalities that restore kidney function rather than with gastrointestinal adsorbents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1797
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both therapeutic agents that may be used to treat gastrointestinal disorders (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ) as well as diarrhea (الإِسْهال). In addition, alosetron is recognized as a medication that can be employed in the management of diarrhea. Together, these drugs provide clinicians with multiple options for addressing diarrheal symptoms and broader intestinal disturbances, supporting comprehensive care for patients with gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 423
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat both الإسهال (diarrhea) and اضطراب معيدي (gastrointestinal disorder). In addition, isopropamide iodide is employed in the management of الإسهال (diarrhea). Together, these medications provide options for alleviating acute and chronic disturbances of the gastrointestinal tract, highlighting their relevance in the treatment of diarrheal illnesses and broader gastrointestinal dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 412
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب الكلية (kidney disorder), indicating that its use should be avoided in individuals with impaired renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2621
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerulopathy) is a disorder affecting the glomeruli of the kidneys, and it can progress to اليوريمية (uremia), a condition characterized by the accumulation of nitrogenous waste products in the blood due to impaired renal filtration. This connection highlights that damage to the kidney’s filtering units may lead to systemic toxicity, emphasizing the need for early identification and treatment of glomerular disease to prevent the development of uremic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1283
retrieved_docs.content: Antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to treat or prevent anemia, and both folic acid and vitamin B12 are specific types of these preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both indicated for the treatment of gastrointestinal disturbances (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ). However, each of these bismuth compounds is contraindicated in patients who have atherosclerotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري), meaning they should not be used in individuals with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1951
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS are classified as liver therapy agents with lipotropic properties, meaning they are used to support hepatic function and promote the metabolism and transport of fats within the liver.
